papers.title: Massive runaway star HD 254577: the pre-supernova binary companion to the progenitor of the supernova remnant IC 443
papers.author_names: B. Dinçel G. Paylı S. K. Yerli A. Ankay R. Neuhäuser M. Mugrauer S. Sheth S. Buder S. Hüttel F. Edelmann K.-U. Michel J. Bätz

abstract.text: The secondaries of the massive binary systems can be found as runaway stars after being ejected due to the supernova (SN) of the more massive component. We search for such stars inside the supernova remnants (SNRs), where a recent SN is guaranteed to have happened. In this paper, we present the massive runaway star HD~254577 as the pre-supernova binary companion to the progenitor of the supernova remnant IC~443 and the neutron star (NS) CXOU~J61705.3$+$222127. We performed spectroscopic observations of the runaway star and specified its atmospheric parameters. Together with the precise \\\\textit{Gaia} DR3 astrometry and photometry, we identified the possible birth origin of the runaway star, and by isochrone fitting, we determined the progenitor mass. From the \\\\textit{Gaia} DR3 proper motions, we specified the possible explosion sites and calculated the neutron star (NS) velocity. HD~254577 is a hot and evolved star with an effective temperature of $24000\\\\pm1000$ K (B0.5II) and a surface gravity of $\\\\log({g~\\\\rm[cm/s^2]})=2.75\\\\pm0.25$. It is probably a single star with a peculiar 3-D velocity of $31.3^{+1.2}_{-0.9}$ km~s$^{-1}$, lying at a heliocentric distance of $1701^{+55}_{-54}$ pc. The cometary tail of the NS implies that it is moving away from the same site as the runaway star. From the flight trajectories, we calculated that the NS has typical pulsar velocities such as $254-539$ km~s$^{-1}$ at a distance of $1.7$ kpc for $10-20$ kyr ages. Together with the blue-only shifted interstellar medium lines on its spectrum, HD~254577 must be the pre-supernova binary companion to the progenitor of IC~443. By identifying the pre-supernova companion and the possible parent cluster, we showed that the progenitor zero-age main sequence mass is high: $31-64$ M$_\\\\odot$. We also discuss the expansion dynamics of the SNR due to the highly off-centered explosion site.

brief.tldr: [research paper] The paper identifies HD 254577 as a massive runaway star with a B0.5II spectral type and effective temperature of 24,000 K, exhibiting a peculiar 3D velocity of 31.3 km s⁻¹ and a heliocentric distance of 1701 pc. Using Gaia DR3 astrometry and spectroscopic data, it demonstrates that HD 254577 is the pre-supernova binary companion to the progenitor of the supernova remnant IC 443, with the neutron star CXOU J61705.2+222127 showing a cometary tail indicating motion away from the same explosion site. Isochrone fitting yields a progenitor zero-age main sequence mass of 31–64 M⊙, supporting a jet-driven explosion scenario, and the off-centered explosion site explains the asymmetric SNR expansion. The findings provide precise SNR distance, constrain binary disruption dynamics, and suggest that while the binary may not have undergone strong mass transfer, the high progenitor mass favors a binary supernova origin.

source_fields.title: Massive runaway star HD 254577: the pre-supernova binary companion to the progenitor of the supernova remnant IC 443

source_fields.sections_text: Summary: HD 254577, a hot, massive, and isolated runaway star located within SNR IC 443, is identified as the pre-supernova binary companion to the SNR's progenitor, with evidence supporting a high-mass binary system that underwent a core-collapse supernova without significant mass loss via a common envelope phase. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel method to identify pre-SN binary companions of runaway stars within supernova remnants by leveraging kinematical and positional correlations, solving the long-standing challenge of accurately determining progenitor binary parameters, explosion centers, and SNR distances in a way that significantly improves upon previous, sparse observational efforts. Observations: This section describes the observational campaign and data reduction procedures used to obtain high-resolution spectroscopic data of the runaway stars HD 254577 and HD 254477, along with archival data for atmospheric parameter determination and supernova remnant filament extension. Results and Discussion: HD 254577 is a hot, evolved B0-type runaway star with $T_{\\mathrm{eff}} = 24000 \\pm 1000$ K, $\\log(g) = 2.75 \\pm 0.25$, $v \\sin i = 150 \\pm 20$ km s$^{-1}$, and a peculiar velocity of $v_{\\mathrm{pec}} = 31.3^{+1.2}_{-0.9}$ km s$^{-1}$, slightly above the runaway threshold, and shows no RV variation indicating it is a single star, while its cluster member HD 254477 exhibits spectroscopic binary behavior. Appendix A Long Table Contents: This section presents a long table listing potential cluster members within 10 parsecs, providing their Gaia DR3 identifiers, geometric distances, parallaxes, photometric properties, and proper motions. IC 443 \\& CXOU J61705.3+222127: IC 443 is a mixed morphology supernova remnant interacting with dense molecular clouds, exhibiting complex morphological and kinematic features consistent with a core-collapse supernova explosion from a massive progenitor and an age of 3–20 kyr. HD 254577 and Neighboring Stars: HD 254577 is a runaway B-type star with a high peculiar velocity, distinct from a nearby young open cluster, indicating it is not a cluster member but rather a dynamically ejected star.

abstract: The secondaries of the massive binary systems can be found as runaway stars after being ejected due to the supernova (SN) of the more massive component.
We search for such stars inside the supernova remnants (SNRs), where a recent SN is guaranteed to have happened.
From the \\\\textit{Gaia} DR3 proper motions, we specified the possible explosion sites and calculated the neutron star (NS) velocity.
It is probably a single star with a peculiar 3-D velocity of $31.3^{+1.2}_{-0.9}$ km~s$^{-1}$, lying at a heliocentric distance of $1701^{+55}_{-54}$ pc.
By identifying the pre-supernova companion and the possible parent cluster, we showed that the progenitor zero-age main sequence mass is high: $31-64$ M$_\\\\odot$.

catalog_assessment.evidence: Paper presents detailed observational analysis of a single runaway star HD 254577, including spectroscopic observations, Gaia DR3 astrometry/photometry, and derived parameters (temperature, velocity, distance).

papers.title: Modeling Stellar Collisions in Galactic Nuclei Using Hydrodynamic Simulations and Machine Learning
papers.author_names: Sanaea C. Rose James C. Lombardi, Jr. Elena González Prieto Fulya Kıroğlu Frederic A. Rasio

abstract.text: Nuclear star clusters represent some of the most extreme collisional environments in the Universe. A typical nuclear star cluster harbors a supermassive black hole at its center, which accelerates stars to high speeds ($\\\\gtrsim 100$-$1000$ km/s) in a region where millions of other stars reside. Direct collisions occur frequently in such high-density environments, where they can shape the stellar populations and drive the evolution of the cluster. We present a suite of a couple hundred high-resolution smoothed-particle hydrodynamics (SPH) simulations of collisions between $1$ M$_\\\\odot$ stars, at impact speeds representative of galactic nuclei. We use our SPH dataset to develop physically-motivated fitting formulae for predicting collision outcomes. While collision-driven mass loss has been examined in detail in the literature, we present a new framework for understanding the effects of "hit-and-run" collisions on a star's trajectory. We demonstrate that the change in stellar velocity follows the tidal-dissipation limit for grazing encounters, while the deflection angle is well-approximated by point-particle dynamics for periapses $\\\\gtrsim0.3$ times the stellar radii. We use our SPH dataset to test two machine learning (ML) algorithms, k-Nearest Neighbors and neural networks, for predicting collision outcomes and properties. We find that the neural network out-performs k-Nearest Neighbors and delivers results on par with and in some cases exceeding the accuracy of our fitting formulae. We conclude that both fitting formulae and ML have merits for modeling collisions in dense stellar environments, however ML may prove more effective as the parameter space of initial conditions expands.

triage.label: rule-weak-llm-confirmed
triage.reason: Weak title match: stellar interactions in dense systems LLM confirmed: Paper explicitly models stars accelerated to high speeds (≳100-1000 km/s) in galactic nuclei, which directly concerns high-velocity stellar populations and their dynamics in extreme environments.

brief.skipped_reason: weak rule match; retained with search metadata only

source_fields.sections_text: Conclusion: This study uses hydrodynamical simulations to characterize stellar collision outcomes in nuclear star clusters, deriving fitting formulae for the capture boundary, fractional mass loss, and kinematic changes of remnants as functions of impact parameter and relative velocity. Introduction: This paper introduces a comprehensive hydrodynamics-based framework that systematically models the diverse outcomes of high-speed stellar collisions near supermassive black holes, overcoming prior assumptions by accounting for realistic initial conditions such as impact parameter, mass ratio, and stellar structure to accurately predict collision results and their implications for stellar populations and transients. Fitting Formulae: This section develops physically motivated fitting formulae to quantify stellar collision outcomes, with a focus on the capture radius and fractional mass loss as functions of impact velocity and periapsis distance. Machine Learning: This section compares machine learning models—specifically k-Nearest Neighbors and neural networks—to physically motivated methods for classifying and regressing collision outcomes in a two-dimensional parameter space, demonstrating their efficacy through optimized hyperparameter tuning and rigorous validation. Qualitative Results: Increasing relative velocity $v_{\\infty}$ in collisions between equal-mass stars leads to a transition from stellar merger (low $v_{\\infty}$) to hit-and-run collisions (intermediate $v_{\\infty}$) to complete destruction (high $v_{\\infty}$ with small impact parameter), with mass loss fractions $f_{\\rm ML}$ exceeding 100% marking the zero-star outcome. Smoothed-particle Hydrodynamics Simulations: We perform smoothed-particle hydrodynamics simulations of hyperbolic collisions between two 1 M⊙ stars to study the outcomes of varying impact velocities and closest approach distances, using an adaptive SPH method with self-gravity, artificial viscosity, and a mechanical energy-based boundness criterion to determine final bound masses and collision-induced velocity changes.

catalog_assessment.evidence: Purely theoretical/simulation paper using hydrodynamic SPH simulations and machine learning to model stellar collisions in galactic nuclei. No observational data or catalog of real stars presented.
